message.content:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - Policy management system for magical items insurance
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: The system supports two operations: quote (compute premium) and claim (process damage). See prompt.md for full specification including item values, premium modifiers, claim processing rules, CLI interface, and integration examples.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

content.text: I'll start by reading the specification file to understand all the requirements.

content.content: No files found

1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### Schema example
237	
238	Stdin:
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
243	  "steps": [
244	    {
245	      "op": "quote",
246	      "items": [
247	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
248	      ]
249	    },
250	    {
251	      "op": "claim",
252	      "policy": 0,
253	      "incident": {
254	        "cause": "fire",
255	        "damages": [
256	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
257	        ]
258	      }
259	    }
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Stdout shape:
265	
266	```json
267	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
268	```
269	
270	## Expected Output Files
271	
272	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
273	- Test files
274	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
275	
276	## Constraints
277	
278	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
279	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
280	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
281	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
282

input.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office -- Example Mapping

## Rules

### R1 -- Item values and base premiums
- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G insurance value, 60 G base premium
- Staff: 800 G insurance value, 80 G base premium
- Potion: 400 G insurance value, 40 G base premium
- Component (rune, moonstone, etc.): 250 G insurance value, 25 G base premium each
- Building block of exactly 3 alike components: 60 G base premium (instead of 75 G)

### R2 -- "Alike" components (for building block discount)
- "Alike" means exactly the same type (e.g. 3 runes, not 2 runes + 1 moonstone)
- Each group of exactly 3 alike components forms one block at 60 G
- Counts that are not exactly 3 do NOT get the block discount (4 runes = 4 x 25 = 100 G)

### R3 -- Premium modifiers (item-specific)
- Cursed: +50% of that item's base premium
- High enchantment (enchantment >= 5): +30% of that item's base premium
- Both can apply to the same item (they stack additively on the item's base)

### R4 -- Premium modifiers (policy-wide, applied to sum of all item base premiums)
- Loyalty discount (customer years >= 2): -20% of policy base premium
- First insurance surcharge: +10% of policy base premium (always applies -- every item in a quote is treated as first insurance regardless of customer history)
- Follow-up contract discount (each contract after the customer's first quote in the scenario): -15% of policy base premium

### R5 -- Processing fee
- 5 G added at the very end, after all modifiers

### R6 -- Modifier application order
- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to each item's base premium individually
- Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up) apply to the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums before item-specific modifiers -- NO, see integration example)
- Actually from the integration examples: policy-wide modifiers apply to the policy base premium which is the sum of item base premiums (the raw base premiums, not including item-specific surcharges). Wait, let me re-read.
- Integration example 1: 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first-insurance = 160 + 5 fee = 165.
  The first-insurance is 10% of 100 (the base), not 10% of 150 (base+curse). So policy-wide modifiers apply to the policy base premium (sum of raw item base premiums).
- Integration example 2: 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench - 20 loyalty + 10 first-insurance - 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee = 160.
  - curse = 50% of 100 = 50. high-ench = 30% of 100 = 30. loyalty = 20% of 100 = 20. first-ins = 10% of 100 = 10. follow-up = 15% of 100 = 15.
  - All modifiers (item-specific and policy-wide) are percentages of the item's/policy's base premium, then summed.

### R7 -- Rounding
- All amounts rounded to whole G in MHPCO's favor
- Premium: rounded UP (ceil) -- 197.5 -> 198
- Payout: rounded DOWN (floor) -- 350.5 -> 350
- Intermediate amounts kept as fractions; only final result is rounded

### R8 -- Claim: deductible
- 100 G deductible per damaged item (per entry in the damages array)

### R9 -- Claim: high enchantment reimbursement
- Items with enchantment >= 8: reimbursed at 50% of damage amount (before deductible)
- Then deductible is subtracted

### R10 -- Claim: dragon material reimbursement
- Items made of dragon material: fully reimbursed (before deductible)
- Then deductible is subtracted

### R11 -- Claim: enchantment >= 8 AND dragon material
- Both clauses apply; the 50% rule wins (more restrictive)
- Then deductible is subtracted
- Example: dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 -> 50% of 1000 = 500, minus 100 deductible = 400

### R12 -- Claim: cap
- Total payout per policy capped at 2x the insurance sum
- Insurance sum = sum of insurance values of all insured items (unmodified by premium modifiers)
- Component block discount affects premium only, not insurance sum
- Cap is tracked across multiple claims on the same policy

### R13 -- Claim: multiple items of same type
- Policy can cover multiple items of the same type
- Each damage entry is treated as a separate damage with its own deductible
- If damages array has more entries of a given type than the policy covers, the whole claim is rejected (non-zero exit code)

### R14 -- CLI interface
- Executable named `claim-office`
- Reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout
- Entry point: src/cli.ts
- Input: { customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: number }, steps: Step[] }
- Step (quote): { op: "quote", items: Item[] }
- Step (claim): { op: "claim", policy: number (zero-based step index of the quote), incident: { cause: string, damages: Damage[] } }
- Item: { type: string, material: string, enchantment: number, cursed: boolean }
- Damage: { itemType: string, amount: number }
- Output: { results: Result[] }
- Quote result: { premium: number }
- Claim result: { payout: number, remainingCap: number }

### R15 -- Error handling
- Unknown item type in quote -> non-zero exit, error to stderr, no results to stdout
- Damage to item not in policy -> non-zero exit, error to stderr
- Unknown item type in damage -> non-zero exit, error to stderr
- Negative damage amount -> non-zero exit, error to stderr

### R16 -- Empty item list
- Empty items in quote -> premium 5 G (processing fee only)

### R17 -- Follow-up contract
- "First quote" means the customer's first quote step in the scenario
- Subsequent quote steps in the same scenario get the -15% follow-up discount
- The first-insurance surcharge (+10%) always applies regardless

## Examples

### Component base premiums
- 2 runes -> 50 G (2 x 25)
- 3 runes -> 60 G (block of 3 alike)
- 4 runes -> 100 G (4 x 25, no block)
- 7 runes -> 175 G (7 x 25, no block)
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone -> 75 G (3 x 25, no block because different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones -> 120 G (two blocks of 3: 60 + 60)

### Modifier scope
- Cursed sword (100 G) + plain amulet (60 G) -> 160 G policy base; curse adds 50 G (50% of 100) -> 210 G before policy-wide modifiers and fee

### Modifier thresholds
- Customer with exactly 2 years -> loyalty discount applies
- Sword with enchantment exactly 5 -> high-enchantment surcharge applies
- Sword with enchantment 5, cursed -> both surcharges apply
- Sword with enchantment 4 -> no high-enchantment surcharge
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 8, damage 1000 -> payout 400 (50% of 1000 = 500, minus 100 deductible)

### Deductible per damage event
- Dragon attack damages sword (500 G) and amulet (300 G) -> payout 600 G (500-100 + 300-100 = 400+200)

### Standard reimbursement
- Regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 -> payout 400 (500 - 100)
- Rune, damage 200 -> payout 100 (200 - 100)

### Enchantment vs dragon material
- Dragon-material sword, ench 9, damage 1000 -> payout 400 (50% -> 500, minus 100)
- Dragon-material sword, ench 5, damage 800 -> payout 700 (full reimburse 800, minus 100)
- Steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 -> payout 400 (50% -> 500, minus 100)

### Multiple items same type
- Two swords -> insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G
- Damage to both -> each gets its own deductible
- More damage entries than insured items of that type -> reject

### Cap exhaustion
- Sword + amulet -> insurance sum 1600 G, cap 3200 G
- Cursed sword (ins value 1000 G) -> cap 2000 G (cap based on unmodified insurance value)
- Sword + 3 runes -> insurance sum 1750 G (1000 + 3x250); block discount affects premium only
- Sword insured (ins sum 1000, cap 2000); two claims of 1500 each:
  - First: payout 1400, remaining 600
  - Second: payout 600, remaining 0

### Rounding
- Premium 197.5 -> 198 (ceil)
- Payout 350.5 -> 350 (floor)

### Edge cases
- Empty item list -> premium 5 G
- Unknown item type in quote -> non-zero exit, stderr error
- Damage to item not in policy -> non-zero exit, stderr error
- Negative damage amount -> non-zero exit, stderr error

### Integration example 1: Newcomer with cursed sword
- Customer: 0 years, no previous contract
- Item: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- Premium: 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first-insurance = 160 + 5 fee = 165 G

### Integration example 2: Long-standing customer's second contract
- Customer: 3 years; this is the customer's second quote
- Item: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- Premium: 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench - 20 loyalty + 10 first-insurance - 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee = 160 G

## Questions / Clarifications

### Q1 (Alike components)
"Alike" means exactly the same component type. 2 runes + 1 moonstone = 3 individual components, no block discount. 3 runes + 3 moonstones = two blocks (60 + 60 = 120).

### Q2 (Modifier scope on multi-item policies)
Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the individual item's base premium. Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up) apply to the policy base premium (sum of all items' base premiums). Fee added last.

### Q3 (Multiple items of same type)
Allowed. Each gets its own insurance value, its own deductible on damage. If damages array has more entries of a type than insured, the whole claim is rejected.

### Q4 (First insurance vs follow-up)
"First insurance" surcharge (+10%) always applies to every quote -- it means each item is being insured for the first time. "Follow-up contract" discount (-15%) applies to the customer's second and subsequent quotes in the scenario. Both can apply simultaneously. A long-standing customer with a new item still gets the first-insurance surcharge AND the follow-up discount (if not their first quote).

## Per-test rationale

1. **empty item list -> premium 5 G**: R16 -- simplest possible quote, only processing fee
2. **single sword -> premium 115 G**: R1, R4 (first-insurance), R5 -- basic item with first-insurance surcharge and fee (100 + 10 + 5)
3. **single amulet -> premium 71 G**: R1 -- verifies amulet pricing (60 + 6 + 5)
4. **single staff -> premium 93 G**: R1 -- verifies staff pricing (80 + 8 + 5)
5. **single potion -> premium 49 G**: R1 -- verifies potion pricing (40 + 4 + 5)
6. **2 runes -> base premium 50 G**: R1 -- component pricing without block
7. **3 runes -> base premium 60 G**: R1, R2 -- block of 3 alike
8. **4 runes -> base premium 100 G**: R2 -- no block for non-3 counts
9. **7 runes -> base premium 175 G**: R2 -- no block for non-3 counts
10. **2 runes + 1 moonstone -> base premium 75 G**: Q1 -- different types, no block
11. **3 runes + 3 moonstones -> base premium 120 G**: Q1 -- two separate blocks
12. **cursed sword -> premium 165 G**: R3 -- integration example 1 (newcomer)
13. **sword enchantment 5 -> high-enchantment surcharge applies**: R3 -- threshold exactly 5
14. **sword enchantment 4 -> no high-enchantment surcharge**: R3 -- below threshold
15. **sword enchantment 5, cursed -> both surcharges**: R3 -- both modifiers stack
16. **cursed sword + plain amulet -> 210 G before policy-wide and fee**: Q2 -- modifier scope
17. **customer 2 years -> loyalty discount applies**: R4 -- threshold exactly 2
18. **customer 1 year -> no loyalty discount**: R4 -- below threshold
19. **follow-up contract gets -15% discount**: R4, R17 -- second quote in scenario
20. **first-insurance always applies even on follow-up**: Q4 -- both modifiers simultaneously
21. **integration: long-standing customer second contract -> 160 G**: full integration example 2
22. **rounding premium up: 197.5 -> 198**: R7
23. **rounding payout down: 350.5 -> 350**: R7
24. **standard claim: regular sword, damage 500 -> payout 400**: R8 -- basic deductible
25. **claim on rune: damage 200 -> payout 100**: R8 -- component claim with deductible
26. **deductible per item: sword 500 + amulet 300 -> payout 600**: R8 -- multiple damages, one deductible each
27. **high enchantment claim: steel sword ench 9, damage 1000 -> payout 400**: R9 -- 50% then deductible
28. **dragon material claim: ench 5, damage 800 -> payout 700**: R10 -- full reimburse then deductible
29. **dragon + high ench: ench 9, damage 1000 -> payout 400**: R11 -- 50% wins
30. **dragon material sword, ench 8, damage 1000 -> payout 400**: R9, R11 -- threshold ench exactly 8
31. **cap: sword + amulet -> cap 3200 G**: R12
32. **cap: cursed sword -> cap 2000 G (unmodified insurance value)**: R12
33. **cap: sword + 3 runes -> insurance sum 1750 G**: R12 -- block discount doesn't affect insurance sum
34. **cap exhaustion: two claims -> first 1400, second capped at 600**: R12 -- multi-claim cap tracking
35. **two swords -> insurance sum 2000, cap 4000**: R13 -- multiple same-type items
36. **two sword damages -> each gets own deductible**: R13
37. **more damages than insured -> reject**: R13, R15
38. **unknown item type in quote -> non-zero exit, stderr**: R15
39. **damage to item not in policy -> non-zero exit, stderr**: R15
40. **negative damage amount -> non-zero exit, stderr**: R15
41. **CLI reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout**: R14
42. **CLI scenario with quote and claim steps**: R14 -- full scenario processing

content.text:
Now let me carefully work through the premium calculations to verify my expected values before writing the test file. Let me double-check a few calculations.

For a single sword (no curse, no special enchantment, newcomer with 0 years, first contract):
- Base premium: 100
- First-insurance surcharge: 10% of 100 = 10
- Fee: 5
- Total: 115 G

For a single amulet (same conditions):
- Base premium: 60
- First-insurance: 10% of 60 = 6
- Fee: 5
- Total: 71 G

For a single staff:
- Base: 80 + 8 + 5 = 93 G

For a single potion:
- Base: 40 + 4 + 5 = 49 G

For single rune (newcomer):
- Base: 25 + 2.5 first-insurance + 5 fee = 32.5 -> rounds up to 33 G

For 2 runes (newcomer):
- Base: 50 + 5 first-insurance + 5 fee = 60 G

For 3 runes block (newcomer):
- Base: 60 + 6 first-insurance + 5 fee = 71 G

Now let me verify the integration examples once more against the spec. Integration example 1 says 165 G, which matches: 100 + 50 + 10 = 160 + 5 = 165. Integration example 2: 100 + 50 + 30 - 20 + 10 - 15 = 155 + 5 = 160.

Now I need to think carefully about test structure. The spec has both unit-level behavior (premium calculation, claim processing) and CLI integration. I'll structure the tests in multiple describe blocks.

Let me think about how to handle the component premium tests. The spec says "2 runes -> 50 G base premium" as base premium only (before policy-wide modifiers and fee). So for those tests, I should test the base premium computation in isolation. But I also need tests that exercise the full premium pipeline including fee.

The most natural approach for TDD: test the `claimOffice` function (or whatever processes the scenario) as a whole. The CLI is a thin wrapper. I'll write tests at two levels:
1. Core logic tests (quote/claim) via the main function
2. CLI tests (stdin/stdout/exit codes)

Let me write the comprehensive test file.

input.old_string:
## Per-test rationale

1. **empty item list -> premium 5 G**: R16 -- simplest possible quote, only processing fee
2. **single sword -> premium 115 G**: R1, R4 (first-insurance), R5 -- basic item with first-insurance surcharge and fee (100 + 10 + 5)
3. **single amulet -> premium 71 G**: R1 -- verifies amulet pricing (60 + 6 + 5)
4. **single staff -> premium 93 G**: R1 -- verifies staff pricing (80 + 8 + 5)
5. **single potion -> premium 49 G**: R1 -- verifies potion pricing (40 + 4 + 5)
6. **2 runes -> base premium 50 G**: R1 -- component pricing without block
7. **3 runes -> base premium 60 G**: R1, R2 -- block of 3 alike
8. **4 runes -> base premium 100 G**: R2 -- no block for non-3 counts
9. **7 runes -> base premium 175 G**: R2 -- no block for non-3 counts
10. **2 runes + 1 moonstone -> base premium 75 G**: Q1 -- different types, no block
11. **3 runes + 3 moonstones -> base premium 120 G**: Q1 -- two separate blocks
12. **cursed sword -> premium 165 G**: R3 -- integration example 1 (newcomer)
13. **sword enchantment 5 -> high-enchantment surcharge applies**: R3 -- threshold exactly 5
14. **sword enchantment 4 -> no high-enchantment surcharge**: R3 -- below threshold
15. **sword enchantment 5, cursed -> both surcharges**: R3 -- both modifiers stack
16. **cursed sword + plain amulet -> 210 G before policy-wide and fee**: Q2 -- modifier scope
17. **customer 2 years -> loyalty discount applies**: R4 -- threshold exactly 2
18. **customer 1 year -> no loyalty discount**: R4 -- below threshold
19. **follow-up contract gets -15% discount**: R4, R17 -- second quote in scenario
20. **first-insurance always applies even on follow-up**: Q4 -- both modifiers simultaneously
21. **integration: long-standing customer second contract -> 160 G**: full integration example 2
22. **rounding premium up: 197.5 -> 198**: R7
23. **rounding payout down: 350.5 -> 350**: R7
24. **standard claim: regular sword, damage 500 -> payout 400**: R8 -- basic deductible
25. **claim on rune: damage 200 -> payout 100**: R8 -- component claim with deductible
26. **deductible per item: sword 500 + amulet 300 -> payout 600**: R8 -- multiple damages, one deductible each
27. **high enchantment claim: steel sword ench 9, damage 1000 -> payout 400**: R9 -- 50% then deductible
28. **dragon material claim: ench 5, damage 800 -> payout 700**: R10 -- full reimburse then deductible
29. **dragon + high ench: ench 9, damage 1000 -> payout 400**: R11 -- 50% wins
30. **dragon material sword, ench 8, damage 1000 -> payout 400**: R9, R11 -- threshold ench exactly 8
31. **cap: sword + amulet -> cap 3200 G**: R12
32. **cap: cursed sword -> cap 2000 G (unmodified insurance value)**: R12
33. **cap: sword + 3 runes -> insurance sum 1750 G**: R12 -- block discount doesn't affect insurance sum
34. **cap exhaustion: two claims -> first 1400, second capped at 600**: R12 -- multi-claim cap tracking
35. **two swords -> insurance sum 2000, cap 4000**: R13 -- multiple same-type items
36. **two sword damages -> each gets own deductible**: R13
37. **more damages than insured -> reject**: R13, R15
38. **unknown item type in quote -> non-zero exit, stderr**: R15
39. **damage to item not in policy -> non-zero exit, stderr**: R15
40. **negative damage amount -> non-zero exit, stderr**: R15
41. **CLI reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout**: R14
42. **CLI scenario with quote and claim steps**: R14 -- full scenario processing
43. **CLI error exits with non-zero status**: R14, R15
input.new_string:
## Per-test rationale

Each line maps a test (by its `it.todo()` description) to the rule(s) or example(s) it covers.

### Quote: base cases
1. **"should return premium 5 G for an empty item list (processing fee only)"** -- R16, R5: empty list, only fee
2. **"should return premium 115 G for a single plain sword"** -- R1, R4, R5: 100 base + 10 first-ins + 5 fee
3. **"should return premium 71 G for a single plain amulet"** -- R1: 60 base + 6 first-ins + 5 fee
4. **"should return premium 93 G for a single plain staff"** -- R1: 80 base + 8 first-ins + 5 fee
5. **"should return premium 49 G for a single plain potion"** -- R1: 40 base + 4 first-ins + 5 fee

### Quote: components and building blocks
6. **"should compute base premium 50 G for 2 runes"** -- R1: 2 x 25
7. **"should compute base premium 60 G for 3 runes"** -- R1, R2: block of 3 alike
8. **"should compute base premium 100 G for 4 runes"** -- R2: no block (not exactly 3)
9. **"should compute base premium 175 G for 7 runes"** -- R2: no block
10. **"should compute base premium 75 G for 2 runes + 1 moonstone"** -- Q1: different types, no block
11. **"should compute base premium 120 G for 3 runes + 3 moonstones"** -- Q1: two separate blocks

### Quote: item-specific modifiers
12. **"should add 50% curse surcharge -- cursed sword newcomer: ... 165 G"** -- R3, integration example 1
13. **"should add 30% high-enchantment surcharge for sword with enchantment exactly 5"** -- R3: threshold
14. **"should NOT add high-enchantment surcharge for sword with enchantment 4"** -- R3: below threshold
15. **"should apply both curse and high-enchantment surcharges"** -- R3: stacking
16. **"should apply item-specific modifiers only to the affected item"** -- Q2: modifier scope

### Quote: policy-wide modifiers
17. **"should apply 20% loyalty discount for customer with exactly 2 years"** -- R4: loyalty threshold
18. **"should NOT apply loyalty discount for customer with 1 year"** -- R4: below threshold
19. **"should apply 10% first-insurance surcharge on every quote"** -- R4: always applies
20. **"should apply 15% follow-up contract discount on second quote"** -- R4, R17: follow-up
21. **"should apply first-insurance surcharge even on follow-up contracts"** -- Q4: both coexist

### Quote: integration examples
22. **"should return 165 G for newcomer ... with cursed sword"** -- full integration example 1
23. **"should return 160 G for long-standing customer ... second contract"** -- full integration example 2

### Quote: rounding
24. **"should round premium UP in MHPCO's favor -- 197.5 G rounds to 198 G"** -- R7

### Claim: standard reimbursement
25. **"should return payout 400 G for regular sword ... damage 500 G"** -- R8: standard, deductible only
26. **"should return payout 100 G for rune, damage 200 G"** -- R8: component claim

### Claim: deductible
27. **"should apply 100 G deductible per damaged item -- sword 500 + amulet 300 = 600"** -- R8: per-item deductible

### Claim: high enchantment
28. **"should reimburse at 50% for steel sword with enchantment 9"** -- R9: 50% then deductible
29. **"should reimburse at 50% for dragon-material sword with enchantment 8"** -- R9, R11: threshold at 8

### Claim: dragon material
30. **"should fully reimburse dragon-material sword with enchantment 5"** -- R10: full then deductible

### Claim: enchantment vs dragon material interaction
31. **"should apply 50% rule when dragon-material sword has enchantment 9"** -- R11: 50% wins

### Claim: rounding
32. **"should round payout DOWN in MHPCO's favor -- 350.5 G rounds to 350 G"** -- R7

### Claim: cap
33. **"should set cap at 2x insurance sum -- sword + amulet, cap 3200 G"** -- R12
34. **"should base cap on unmodified insurance value -- cursed sword, cap 2000 G"** -- R12
35. **"should not let block discount affect insurance sum -- sword + 3 runes = 1750 G"** -- R12
36. **"should exhaust cap across multiple claims"** -- R12: multi-claim tracking

### Claim: multiple items of same type
37. **"should allow two swords in a policy -- insurance sum 2000, cap 4000"** -- R13, Q3
38. **"should apply separate deductible per damage entry when both swords damaged"** -- R13
39. **"should reject claim if damages exceed insured item count"** -- R13, R15

### Error handling
40. **"should reject quote with unknown item type"** -- R15: e.g. broomstick
41. **"should reject claim referencing damage item not in policy"** -- R15
42. **"should reject claim with negative damage amount"** -- R15

### CLI integration

content.text:
The test list is complete. Here is the summary.

---

```
Test List Created:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office
Test File: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Tests: 48

Test Cases (ordered simple -> complex):

Quote: base cases
 1. should return premium 5 G for an empty item list (processing fee only)
 2. should return premium 115 G for a single plain sword (100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee)
 3. should return premium 71 G for a single plain amulet (60 base + 6 first-insurance + 5 fee)
 4. should return premium 93 G for a single plain staff (80 base + 8 first-insurance + 5 fee)
 5. should return premium 49 G for a single plain potion (40 base + 4 first-insurance + 5 fee)

Quote: components and building blocks
 6. should compute base premium 50 G for 2 runes (2 x 25, no block)
 7. should compute base premium 60 G for 3 runes (block of 3 alike applies)
 8. should compute base premium 100 G for 4 runes (no block -- block requires exactly 3)
 9. should compute base premium 175 G for 7 runes (7 x 25, no block)
10. should compute base premium 75 G for 2 runes + 1 moonstone (no block: different types)
11. should compute base premium 120 G for 3 runes + 3 moonstones (two separate blocks: 60 + 60)

Quote: item-specific modifiers
12. should add 50% curse surcharge -- cursed sword newcomer: 165 G
13. should add 30% high-enchantment surcharge for sword with enchantment exactly 5
14. should NOT add high-enchantment surcharge for sword with enchantment 4
15. should apply both curse and high-enchantment surcharges to sword with enchantment 5, cursed
16. should apply item-specific modifiers only to the affected item -- cursed sword + plain amulet

Quote: policy-wide modifiers
17. should apply 20% loyalty discount for customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO
18. should NOT apply loyalty discount for customer with 1 year with MHPCO
19. should apply 10% first-insurance surcharge on every quote (always applies)
20. should apply 15% follow-up contract discount on the customer's second quote
21. should apply first-insurance surcharge even on follow-up contracts -- both modifiers coexist

Quote: integration examples
22. should return 165 G for newcomer with cursed sword (integration example 1)
23. should return 160 G for long-standing customer's second contract (integration example 2)

Quote: rounding
24. should round premium UP in MHPCO's favor -- 197.5 G rounds to 198 G

Claim: standard reimbursement
25. should return payout 400 G for regular sword, damage 500 G
26. should return payout 100 G for rune, damage 200 G

Claim: deductible
27. should apply 100 G deductible per damaged item -- sword 500 + amulet 300 = payout 600 G

Claim: high enchantment
28. should reimburse at 50% for steel sword with enchantment 9 -- payout 400 G
29. should reimburse at 50% for dragon-material sword with enchantment 8 -- payout 400 G

Claim: dragon material
30. should fully reimburse dragon-material sword with enchantment 5 -- payout 700 G

Claim: enchantment vs dragon material interaction
31. should apply 50% rule when dragon-material sword has enchantment 9 -- payout 400 G

Claim: rounding
32. should round payout DOWN in MHPCO's favor -- 350.5 G rounds to 350 G

Claim: cap
33. should set cap at 2x insurance sum -- sword + amulet, cap 3200 G
34. should base cap on unmodified insurance value -- cursed sword, cap 2000 G
35. should not let block discount affect insurance sum -- sword + 3 runes = 1750 G
36. should exhaust cap across multiple claims -- first 1400, second capped at 600

Claim: multiple items of same type
37. should allow two swords in a policy -- insurance sum 2000, cap 4000
38. should apply separate deductible per damage entry when both swords damaged
39. should reject claim if damages exceed insured item count

Error handling
40. should reject quote with unknown item type (e.g. broomstick)
41. should reject claim referencing damage item not in policy
42. should reject claim with negative damage amount

CLI integration
43. should read JSON scenario from stdin and write JSON results to stdout
44. should process a full scenario with quote and claim steps
45. should exit with non-zero status code for unknown item type
46. should exit with non-zero status code for damage to item not in policy
47. should exit with non-zero status code for negative damage amount
48. should exit with non-zero status code when damages exceed insured item count

Next Step: Proceed to Red phase to activate the first test.
```
